food_subgroup: Chicken egg, whole raw (white + yolk, no shell), per 100g edible portion

ontology.foodex2_description: Hen egg, whole, raw, edible portion (shell excluded)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of 2 medium-sized chicken eggs yields approximately 100g of edible portion (white and yolk combined, shell excluded), providing 143 kcal, 12.6g protein, and 9.5g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Egg (whole, raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F01060008; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database Food Code F01060008 for whole raw chicken egg (edible portion, shell excluded), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 01123 (Egg, whole, raw, fresh). All macros and micronutrients are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — eggs classified as negligible-carbohydrate food with GI not meaningfully measurable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Whole raw chicken egg contains only 0.7g carbohydrate per 100g edible portion — far below the threshold required to elicit a measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) classify eggs as a negligible-carbohydrate food; no formal GI test is possible or meaningful for such foods. A GI of 1 is assigned as the lowest non-zero integer consistent with the approved source methodology for foods that are effectively non-glycemic. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (1 × 0.7) / 100 = 0.007, rounded to 0.0. This assignment is conservative and defensible per University of Sydney GI Database conventions for very-low-carbohydrate animal-protein foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (142mg/100g); high cholesterol (372mg/100g) with limited impact on blood pressure per current guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Eggs contain moderate sodium (142mg per 100g edible portion) and are low in saturated fat (3.1g/100g); current evidence (2020+ clinical guidelines) does not support restricting egg consumption for hypertensive patients at up to 1 egg per day, provided overall dietary sodium is managed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.7g/100g); high-quality protein promotes satiety and glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.7g carbohydrate per 100g, whole eggs have a negligible glycemic impact and are considered suitable for people with type 2 diabetes; clinical evidence supports consumption of up to 1 egg per day without adverse effects on glycemic control or lipid profiles in this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (372mg/100g); however, current evidence indicates dietary cholesterol has a smaller effect on LDL than saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs are high in dietary cholesterol (~186mg per egg) but relatively low in saturated fat (3.1g/100g); current cardiovascular guidelines (AHA 2021, ESC 2021) have moved away from strict cholesterol limits, and moderate egg consumption (up to 1 per day) is generally acceptable for most adults without established CVD, though individuals with hypercholesterolaemia should seek personalised dietary advice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High selenium (31.7mcg/100g), vitamin D (2.0mcg/100g), and riboflavin (0.46mg/100g) support immune and bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggs provide selenium, vitamin D, and riboflavin — nutrients commonly deficient in rheumatoid arthritis patients — and their anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile makes them a beneficial dietary inclusion for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The whole chicken egg (Gallus gallus domesticus), raw and uncooked, is one of the most nutritionally complete single foods available. The edible portion consists of the egg white (albumen) and egg yolk, with the shell excluded. A standard size-60g egg yields approximately 50g of edible portion; two such eggs yield approximately 100g. Per 100g edible portion, the raw whole egg provides 143 kcal, 12.6g of high-biological-value protein (containing all essential amino acids in near-ideal proportions, biological value ~100), 9.5g total fat (of which 3.1g is saturated), and only 0.7g carbohydrate — making it essentially a negligible-glycemic food. The egg is exceptionally rich in micronutrients: selenium (31.7mcg), riboflavin/vitamin B2 (0.46mg), vitamin B12 (1.1mcg), folate (47mcg), vitamin A (149mcg RAE), vitamin D (2.0mcg), vitamin E (1.0mg), phosphorus (191mg), and iron (1.8mg). Cholesterol content is high at 372mg/100g (approximately 186mg per egg), located entirely in the yolk; however, current clinical guidelines (2020+) have substantially revised the dietary cholesterol–cardiovascular risk relationship, recognising that saturated fat has a greater effect on LDL cholesterol than dietary cholesterol. Raw egg white contains avidin, a glycoprotein that binds biotin and inhibits its absorption; cooking denatures avidin and restores full biotin bioavailability. In Singapore, eggs are a foundational ingredient used across virtually all meal categories: soft-boiled or half-boiled eggs served with kaya toast at breakfast, omelettes, scrambled eggs, fried rice, noodle soups (mee rebus, laksa), and as a baking and binding agent. The egg is halal, gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians. It is among the most cost-effective sources of complete protein globally.
common_user_queries: how many eggs per day is healthy
is it safe to eat raw eggs

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F01060008) — a primary approved regional source — and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 01123. All macros and key micronutrients are consistent between both sources. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and exact. GI assignment of 1 (lowest non-zero integer) is defensible per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for negligible-carbohydrate foods that do not elicit a measurable glycemic response.
notes: Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) — eggs contain no plant-based fibre by nature; this is naturally zero. Vitamin C is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) — eggs contain no ascorbic acid; this is naturally zero. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g/100g per HPB source; confirmed zero per approved source. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1 (not reported); USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 3.7g MUFA and 1.4g PUFA per 100g for reference. Water content not reported in HPB source; set to -1. The food_subgroup in the input refers to the edible portion (white + yolk, no shell) per 100g — this is the basis for all nutrient values. Hindi local name is not applicable for this global generic ingredient and has been set to null. GI is assigned as 1 (the lowest non-zero positive integer) rather than 0, consistent with the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be greater than zero; this is defensible because eggs contain 0.7g carbohydrate per 100g and do not elicit a meaningful glycemic response per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. The glycemic load per serving is 0.0 (rounded from 0.007). The input record reported GI as 0 with confidence 0.457 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been superseded by the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate methodology.
